name: Mymerak gravel route between wine cellars
name_original: Ruta Mymerak gravel entre bodegas de vino
description:
The 80 kilometers of this Route will bring you closer to some of the most famous wineries in La Rioja and the Basque Country. The circular route follows the contours of the southern sierras of Álava to the east and returns to the starting point in Haro following the Ebro river.

You start from Haro and go up for about 13 kilometers towards the Toloño mountain, from where you can admire the views of the Ebro valley. On the descent towards San Vicente de la Sonsierra you pass by the medieval necropolis of the Bardallo graves, a peculiar sample of life in these parts in the 10th century.

On your way east you pass by wineries whose architecture is as important as the quality of their wines: the Baigorri winery and the Ysios winery will not leave you indifferent. It is also worth stopping at the town of La Hoya, an archaeological site that includes a small museum.

In the town of El Ciego there is another sample of spectacular architecture, the Elciego wineries, designed by Frank Gehry. The return to Haro, following the course of the Ebro river, passes by two castles, that of Davalillo and the castle-fortress of San Vicente de la Sonsierra. The last kilometers, very flat and next to the river, take you back to Haro, the end of the Route.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Haro offers routes through the renowned wine region of La Rioja. The terrain is characterized by rolling hills, extensive vineyards, and paths alongside the Ebro River. These routes provide a mix of surfaces, from compacted gravel to quieter country roads, suitable for exploring the agricultural landscape. Best gravel bike trails around Haro The most popular gravel bike trail is Mymerak gravel route between wine cellars, a 51.1 miles (82.2 km) trail that takes 6 hours 59 minutes to complete. This challenging route navigates through the heart of Haro's wine country. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Gravel route through vineyards along the Ebro River, a moderate 24.5 miles (39.5 km) path. This route offers scenic views of vineyards and follows the course of the Ebro River. Local gravel bikers also love the Haro – Haro loop from Haro, a 29.3 miles (47.2 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 18 minutes. Gravel biking around Haro is defined by vineyards, the Ebro River, and rolling hills. The network offers options for easy gravel bike trails Haro, as well as more challenging advanced gravel bike routes Haro. The routes in Haro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 200 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Haro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of gravel bike trails can I find around Haro?
A: Haro's gravel biking landscape is diverse, offering routes through the renowned wine region of La Rioja. You'll find paths winding through extensive vineyards, alongside the Ebro River, and over rolling hills. The surfaces vary from compacted gravel to quieter country roads, providing a mix of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: Are there easy gravel bike trails suitable for beginners in Haro?
A: Yes, Haro offers options for easy gravel bike trails. While many routes involve some elevation, there are paths that are less demanding. For instance, the region has 2 easy tours and 10 moderate tours out of 24 total, ensuring there are suitable options for those new to gravel biking or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What are the typical lengths and durations of gravel bike routes in Haro?
A: Gravel bike routes around Haro vary significantly in length and duration. You can find shorter, moderate rides like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1502633780/gravel-route-through-vineyards-along-the-ebro-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel route through vineyards along the Ebro River&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 24.5 miles (39.5 km) and takes around 3.5 hours. Longer, more challenging routes, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050802839/mymerak-gravel-route-from-haro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mymerak gravel route from Haro&lt;/a&gt;, can be over 44 miles (71 km) and take more than 6.5 hours to complete.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes around Haro?
A: Yes, Haro features several circular gravel bike routes, perfect for starting and ending your ride in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23740633" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haro – Haro loop from Haro&lt;/a&gt;, which covers about 29.3 miles (47.2 km) and typically takes around 3 hours 18 minutes to complete.

Q: What is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Haro?
A: The best time for gravel biking in Haro is generally during spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these seasons, the weather is typically mild and pleasant, ideal for exploring the vineyards and countryside. Summers can be hot, while winters might bring cooler temperatures, though many routes remain accessible year-round.

Q: Can I find advanced or challenging gravel bike routes in Haro?
A: Absolutely. Haro offers a good selection of advanced gravel bike routes for experienced riders. Out of the 24 total tours, 12 are classified as difficult. These routes often feature significant elevation gains and longer distances, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38167089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ezcaray Greenway – Oja Greenway loop from Haro&lt;/a&gt;, which is over 44 miles (71 km) with notable climbs.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or natural attractions to see along the gravel bike trails near Haro?
A: Yes, the region around Haro boasts beautiful natural attractions, including several waterfalls. You might encounter highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5474808" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ollerías Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6864843" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Inglares Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into your gravel biking adventures. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2905300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Water Route&lt;/a&gt; is also a notable trail in the area.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the trails in Haro?
A: The gravel bike trails in Haro are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.1 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 200 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the scenic vineyard views and the unique experience of riding through La Rioja's wine country.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or interesting places to explore near the gravel routes?
A: Beyond the vineyards, Haro and its surroundings are rich in history and culture. You can explore the town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5184637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Haro&lt;/a&gt; itself, known for its wine heritage. Other notable landmarks include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3692683" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Cihuri&lt;/a&gt;, a historic bridge, and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sajazarra Castle&lt;/a&gt;, offering glimpses into the region's past.

Q: Is parking available near the gravel bike trailheads in Haro?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, Haro, as a regional center, offers various public parking options. It's generally advisable to look for parking in or near the town center, or at designated parking areas close to popular starting points for routes. Many routes are accessible directly from Haro.

Q: Are the gravel bike trails in Haro suitable for families?
A: For families, it's best to choose the easier and shorter routes, especially those with less elevation gain. While there are 2 easy tours and 10 moderate tours available, always check the specific route details for distance, elevation, and surface type to ensure it matches your family's capabilities and preferences. The paths along the Ebro River might offer flatter sections suitable for a family outing.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the gravel bike routes?
A: Given Haro's location in a prominent wine region, you'll often find opportunities for refreshments. Many routes pass through or near small villages and towns where cafes, bodegas, or restaurants are available. Planning your route with komoot allows you to identify potential stops and highlights along the way to enjoy local food and drink.
tips: Bodegas López de Heredia Viña Tondonia S.A. occupy a total area of 53,076 m² with 19,718 m² of buildings, of which 3,433, 41 m² are underground wine cellars. One of these cellars was hewn into the rock over a length of 200 m and a depth of 15 m. 12,900 barrique barrels are stored along this gallery.
The Briñas Bridge is a medieval bridge from the 11th century with seven eyes, a pleasure to see and cross.

This necropolis allows us to travel back in time and discover the peculiarities of life in this territory in the 10th century. The isolation of this area kept it away from the incursions of the Visigoths, Muslims and Navarrese. At that time there were no large settlements in the area, only small villages built around sanctuaries or hermitages. They also had burial areas, and it is precisely these necropolises that are the only thing that has come down to us from these settlers.

The Bardallo graves are oriented to the east and to the southeast, alternating tombs of adults and children. The hermitage has disappeared, although its traces can be seen. It is possible that the stones from the hermitage and other blocks from the cemetery were reused, using the place as a quarry.

Super cool architecture of the very famous Bodegas "Baigorri". Most of it is underground down the hill, only the entrance is visible above as a glass cubature.
Tastings, tours and lunch are offered and are highly recommended:
https://bodegasbaigorri.com/en/visit-the-winery/
Incredible build against the backdrop of the mountains. The wine has to bring in so much money that you can take such architects. Calatrava also designed the white bridge in Bilbao.

Between 1200 BC and 250 BC this town was the most important in the area we know today as Rioja Alavesa. The town was structured into streets and squares and was the most populated in the area.

In addition to the excavations of the archaeological site, there is a small museum.
This is a great place to start or end an adventure. From here you have views of all the surroundings below. There is a water fountain and there is a parking very close.

The impressive steel and titanium building, designed by Frank Gehry, has become an icon of the region.
It houses a luxurious hotel and restaurant.
I recommend a guided tour of the building and the cellars, with a wine tasting included.

Documented for the first time in 1367, when Enrique de Trastámara gave it to Carlos El Malo of Navarra, it is likely that it was built during the reign of Alfonso VIII to strengthen the area's defense against Navarre.

It is Romanesque in style and is made up of a walled outer enclosure with a polygonal plan with seven sides. The keep, with four floors, has a square floor plan and is attached to the wall at its eastern end.

The castle is located on a hill next to a meander of the Ebro river, a strategic place next to the Briones castle to protect the area from Navarrese attacks.
It is the typical castle-fortress raised on a hill, an old memory of the early medieval rock castle, with a defensive function.
